content:
# 1 Thessalonians Chapter 1 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/1TH_001_page_001.jpg

---

**1** Paul and Silvanus and Timothy, to the assembly of the Thessalonians in God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ: Grace to you and peace.

**2** We give thanks to God always concerning you all, making mention of you in our prayers unceasingly,

**3** remembering your work of faith and labor of love and endurance of hope in our Lord Jesus Christ, before our God and Father,

**4** knowing, brothers beloved by God, your election,

**5** because our gospel did not come to you in word only, but also in power and in the Holy Spirit and in much assurance, just as you know what manner of men we were among you for your sake.

**6** And you became imitators of us and of the Lord, having received the word in much affliction with joy of the Holy Spirit,

**7** so that you became an example to all the believers in Macedonia and in Achaia.

**8** For from you the word of the Lord has sounded forth, not only in Macedonia and Achaia, but in every place your faith toward God has gone out, so that we have no need to speak anything.

**9** For they themselves report concerning us what manner of entrance we had to you, and how you turned to God from idols, to serve the living and true God,

**10** and to await his Son from the heavens, whom he raised from the dead — Jesus, the one who delivers us from the coming wrath.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

This chapter presents a remarkable degree of agreement among the three translators, which reflects the relative clarity and straightforwardness of Paul's Greek in this opening chapter. Nevertheless, meaningful divergences arise in nearly every verse, and each was carefully adjudicated.

- **v.1:** All three translators correctly followed the shorter Sinaiticus salutation (ΧΑΡΙΣ ΥΜΙΝ ΚΑΙ ΕΙΡΗΝΗ), omitting the longer expansion found in Byzantine manuscripts. EMET and LOGOS both render ΕΚΚΛΗΣΙΑ as "assembly," which SOPHIA adopts; PNEUMA's "church" imports later ecclesiastical connotation. PNEUMA's "From Paul" and "who belong to" are interpretive additions not present in the Greek.

- **v.2:** EMET places "unceasingly" (ΑΔΙΑΛΕΙΠΤΩΣ) at the end of v.2, attached to the prayer-making. LOGOS omits "unceasingly" from v.2, folding it into v.3 as "without ceasing." PNEUMA renders it as "constantly." The placement is syntactically ambiguous in the Greek; SOPHIA follows the reading that connects it to the prayers in v.2, which is the more natural punctuation of the Sinaiticus text.

- **v.3:** The three-fold genitive chain (work of faith, labor of love, endurance of hope) is rendered formally by EMET and LOGOS, preserving the Greek construction. PNEUMA expands each into a clause ("work that springs from faith," etc.), which is vivid but interpretive. SOPHIA preserves the formal parallelism. LOGOS places "without ceasing" at the start of v.3; SOPHIA keeps it in v.2.

- **v.4:** EMET renders ΑΔΕΛΦΟΙ ΗΓΑΠΗΜΕΝΟΙ as "brothers having-been-loved"; LOGOS has "brothers beloved"; PNEUMA expands to "brothers and sisters loved." SOPHIA follows LOGOS. The gender-inclusive expansion, while pastorally motivated, is not in the manuscript text.

- **v.5:** EMET uses "good-news" for ΕΥΑΓΓΕΛΙΟΝ; LOGOS uses "gospel"; PNEUMA uses "good news." SOPHIA adopts "gospel" as the standard formal-equivalent term. EMET's "assurance" and LOGOS's "assurance" agree; PNEUMA's "deep conviction" is interpretive but captures the experiential dimension of ΠΛΗΡΟΦΟΡΙΑ. SOPHIA retains "assurance."

- **v.6:** Close agreement across all three. PNEUMA restructures significantly ("In the midst of severe suffering, you welcomed the message"), converting the participle into a main clause and rendering ΘΛΙΨΙΣ ΠΟΛΛΗ as "severe suffering" rather than "much affliction." SOPHIA follows the EMET/LOGOS participial structure.

- **v.7:** EMET renders ΤΥΠΟΥΣ as "types" (with transliteration); LOGOS and PNEUMA both use "example." PNEUMA notes the Sinaiticus reads the singular ΤΥΠΟΝ. SOPHIA adopts "an example" (singular, following the manuscript). All three agree on the geographic scope (Macedonia and Achaia).

- **v.8:** EMET's "has-sounded-forth" and LOGOS's "has sounded forth" agree closely; PNEUMA's "rang out" captures the acoustic force well. SOPHIA follows LOGOS's formal rendering. PNEUMA's restructured second clause ("Your faith in God has become known everywhere") departs significantly from the Greek structure.

- **v.9:** All three agree on the substance. PNEUMA reverses the Greek word order (turning from idols first, then to God), while the Greek puts the turning toward God first. EMET and LOGOS preserve the Greek order. PNEUMA's "People themselves" replaces the more faithful "they themselves." SOPHIA follows the Greek order with LOGOS.

- **v.10:** EMET renders ΤΟΝ ΡΥΟΜΕΝΟΝ as "the-[one]-delivering"; LOGOS as "the one who delivers"; PNEUMA as "the one who rescues." SOPHIA follows LOGOS's present-tense participial rendering, which preserves the ongoing force of the Greek present participle. For the final phrase, EMET's "the wrath, the-[one]-coming" preserves the double article; LOGOS's "the wrath that is coming" and PNEUMA's "the coming wrath" both capture the sense. SOPHIA adopts "the coming wrath" for its concision while preserving the participial force.

**Manuscript verification:** The Codex Sinaiticus image (page 201) confirms the text of 1 Thessalonians beginning at the top of column 1, with the heading ΠΡΟΣ ΘΕΣΣΑΛΟΝΙΚΕΙΣ Α visible. The scriptio continua proceeds clearly through the four-column format. The nomina sacra are contracted in standard fashion (ΘΥ, ΚΩ, ΙΥ ΧΥ, ΠΝΙ, ΥΝ). The shorter salutation in v.1 is confirmed. The text runs through column 1 and into column 2, consistent with all three translators' observations.

— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA
